data.text: CanESM5 projects a 3.1°C increase in global mean surface temperature by 2100 under the SSP5-8.5 scenario.
The sea ice thickness simulated by CanESM5 in the Arctic declines by 40% between 2020 and 2050.
Precipitation biases in CanESM5 over South Asia are reduced after bias-correction using quantile mapping.
We compare CanESM5 and NorESM2 projections of Atlantic overturning circulation weakening under SSP2-4.5.
CanESM5 overestimates summer precipitation over the Canadian Prairies by approximately 15% relative to observations.
MIROC6 shows stronger warming in the tropics compared to the CMIP5 ensemble mean under the same forcing.
The cloud feedback parameterisation in MIROC6 leads to a higher equilibrium climate sensitivity than MIROC5.
MIROC6 and IPSL-CM6A-LR agree on the sign but differ in the magnitude of projected Arctic warming.
Ocean heat uptake in MIROC6 is consistent with Argo float observations over the 2005–2020 period.
We evaluate MIROC6 against ERA5 reanalysis data to assess upper-tropospheric temperature trends.
MPI-ESM1-2-HR is used to downscale regional precipitation changes over the Mediterranean under SSP3-7.0.
The land surface scheme in MPI-ESM1-2-LR underestimates evapotranspiration over tropical rainforests.
MPI-ESM1-2-HR achieves a nominal horizontal resolution of 100 km in the atmosphere and 40 km in the ocean.
Decadal predictability of North Atlantic sea surface temperatures is higher in MPI-ESM1-2-HR than in lower-resolution variants.
We use MPI-ESM1-2-LR to investigate the role of soil carbon feedbacks under the SSP1-2.6 mitigation scenario.
HadGEM3-GC31-LL provides the highest resolution atmosphere-ocean configuration among the UK Met Office contributions to CMIP6.
The tropical cyclone frequency projected by HadGEM3-GC31-MM is substantially higher than in coarser-resolution models.
HadGEM3-GC31-LL simulates the observed strengthening of the North Atlantic jet stream with greater fidelity than CMIP5 predecessors.
We bias-correct daily temperature output from HadGEM3-GC31-LL using the EWEMBI observational dataset.
HadGEM3-GC31-LL and EC-Earth3-Veg project divergent future changes in European summer blocking frequency.
GFDL-ESM4 includes interactive carbon cycle components that allow prognostic CO2 concentration in fully coupled mode.
The ocean biogeochemistry module in GFDL-ESM4 simulates a 15% decline in primary productivity under SSP5-8.5.
GFDL-ESM4 and GFDL-CM4 share the same atmospheric component but differ in their land and ocean configurations.
Projected changes in Atlantic hurricane intensity from GFDL-ESM4 suggest a 10–15% increase in Category 4–5 storms by 2100.
We assess the performance of GFDL-ESM4 in reproducing the El Niño–Southern Oscillation variability over 1950–2014.
NorESM2-MM is used to examine the radiative forcing attributable to aerosol-cloud interactions over the North Atlantic.
The carbon cycle in NorESM2 shows a stronger land carbon uptake response to elevated CO2 than the CMIP6 median.
NorESM2-LM and NorESM2-MM differ primarily in horizontal resolution, with NorESM2-MM offering a 1° ocean grid.
Sea surface temperature biases in NorESM2 over the Southern Ocean are reduced compared to NorESM1.
We compare historical temperature trends in NorESM2 with HadCRUT5 observational records from 1850 to 2014.
ACCESS-CM2 is the Australian contribution to CMIP6, based on the UM atmosphere model coupled to the MOM5 ocean.
Projected rainfall changes over southern Australia differ substantially between ACCESS-CM2 and the CMIP6 ensemble median.
ACCESS-CM2 exhibits a strong positive cloud shortwave feedback that results in an equilibrium climate sensitivity of 4.7°C.
The representation of the Madden-Julian Oscillation in ACCESS-CM2 is improved relative to its CMIP5 predecessor ACCESS1.3.
We use ACCESS-CM2 output to drive the CABLE land surface model for regional hydrological projections over the Murray-Darling Basin.
IPSL-CM6A-LR is among the models with the highest equilibrium climate sensitivity in CMIP6, estimated at 4.8°C.
The stratospheric ozone representation in IPSL-CM6A-LR is prescribed from climatological observations rather than interactively computed.
IPSL-CM6A-LR projects a 30% increase in extreme precipitation events over Central Europe under SSP3-7.0 by 2080.
We evaluate IPSL-CM6A-LR against GRACE satellite observations for Greenland ice sheet mass balance trends.
Ocean circulation changes in IPSL-CM6A-LR are compared to those in EC-Earth3-CC under identical forcing scenarios.
BCC-CSM2-MR is the Beijing Climate Center contribution to CMIP6, featuring a T106 spectral resolution atmosphere.
BCC-CSM2-MR underestimates the observed warming trend over the Tibetan Plateau by approximately 0.3°C per decade.
The aerosol indirect effect in BCC-CSM2-MR is weaker than in most other CMIP6 models, leading to lower historical warming.
We compare BCC-CSM2-MR and FGOALS-g3 projections of East Asian monsoon precipitation under SSP2-4.5.
INM-CM5-0 has the lowest equilibrium climate sensitivity among CMIP6 models, estimated at 1.8°C.
The sea level rise projected by INM-CM5-0 is substantially lower than the CMIP6 multi-model mean under all SSPs.
INM-CM5-0 shows limited sensitivity of Arctic sea ice to greenhouse gas forcing compared to the ensemble mean.
EC-Earth3 is a coupled atmosphere-ocean model developed by a consortium of European meteorological institutes.
EC-Earth3-Veg includes an interactive vegetation module that allows dynamic changes in plant functional types.
EC-Earth3-CC projects a carbon sink-to-source transition in the Amazon basin by mid-century under SSP5-8.5.
We use EC-Earth3 large ensemble simulations to separate forced climate change signals from internal variability.
EC-Earth3 and HadGEM3-GC31-LL show consistent projections of Sahel precipitation increases under SSP1-2.6.
CESM2 is the Community Earth System Model version 2, developed at the National Center for Atmospheric Research.
The CESM2 large ensemble provides 100 members to quantify the role of internal variability in regional climate projections.
CESM2-WACCM includes a fully interactive stratosphere with chemistry relevant for ozone and UV radiation projections.
Soil moisture trends in CESM2 over North America are sensitive to the choice of land surface parameterisation.
We evaluate CESM2 sea ice area against passive microwave satellite observations over 1979–2014.
CESM2 simulates a 20% reduction in summer Arctic sea ice by 2050 under the SSP2-4.5 scenario.
ERA5 reanalysis provides hourly estimates of atmospheric variables at 31 km horizontal resolution from 1940 to present.
We use ERA5 as observational reference to evaluate the ability of CMIP6 models to reproduce historical warming trends.
ERA5 shows a statistically significant increase in the frequency of atmospheric blocking events over Europe since 1980.
The 2-metre temperature bias of ERA5 over the Tibetan Plateau has been reduced significantly compared to ERA-Interim.
ERA5 precipitation estimates are used to calibrate the convective parameterisation in the regional model RegCM4.
Wind speed trends derived from ERA5 suggest a 5–8% increase in offshore wind energy potential over the North Sea by 2050.
ERA-Interim was the ECMWF reanalysis product widely used until ERA5 became available in 2019.
We compare ERA-Interim and JRA-55 temperature trends over the Southern Ocean for the period 1979–2010.
ERA-Interim overestimates precipitation over the central Amazon compared to TRMM satellite observations.
The transition from ERA-Interim to ERA5 introduced a step change in diagnosed Arctic amplification metrics.
JRA-55 is the Japan Meteorological Agency reanalysis product covering the period from 1958 to the present.
JRA-55 and ERA5 agree well on the sign of stratospheric temperature trends but differ in their magnitudes.
We use JRA-55 as boundary forcing for the CORDEX South Asia regional climate downscaling experiment.

value.text: CORDEX South Asia regional climate downscaling experiment

data.text: MERRA-2 is the Modern-Era Retrospective Analysis for Research and Applications, version 2, produced by NASA.

value.text: Modern-Era Retrospective Analysis for Research and Applications

data.text: MERRA-2 aerosol reanalysis is used to constrain radiative forcing estimates in historical model simulations.
We compare MERRA-2 and ERA5 global precipitable water vapour trends over the period 1980–2020.
NCEP-CFSR provides global atmospheric reanalysis at T382 spectral resolution covering 1979 to 2010.
We use NCEP reanalysis as boundary conditions for the WRF regional climate model over East Asia.
CFSR storm track statistics are compared with those from ERA5 over the North Pacific for the period 1979–2018.
The CORDEX-AFRICA experiment coordinates regional climate downscaling over the African continent using multiple driving GCMs.
CORDEX simulations over South Asia project a 20–30% increase in monsoon precipitation variability under RCP 8.5.
We use six CORDEX-Europe regional models driven by four CMIP5 GCMs to assess uncertainty in future temperature projections.
CORDEX-EAS provides dynamically downscaled projections at 50 km resolution over East Asia for CMIP5 and CMIP6 driving models.
RegCM4 is a regional climate model developed by ICTP, capable of resolving mesoscale atmospheric dynamics at 10–50 km resolution.
We run RegCM4 at 25 km resolution driven by CanESM5 boundary conditions to produce high-resolution projections for West Africa.
RegCM4 overestimates summer precipitation over the Ethiopian Highlands compared to CHIRPS gauge-based observations.
RegCM4 and WRF show different responses of Mediterranean summer temperatures to SST forcing from IPSL-CM6A-LR.
WRF is used in climate mode to dynamically downscale CMIP6 GCM output to 9 km resolution over the contiguous United States.
The WRF model at 4 km convection-permitting resolution captures the observed diurnal cycle of summer precipitation over the Alps.
We use WRF-Chem to simulate aerosol-cloud interactions over South and East Asia driven by MPI-ESM1-2-HR boundary forcing.
WRF projections at 12 km resolution show a 15% increase in extreme precipitation frequency over the western United States by 2070.
Under RCP 2.6, global mean surface temperatures are projected to stabilise below 2°C above pre-industrial levels by 2100.
RCP 4.5 represents a stabilisation scenario in which radiative forcing reaches 4.5 W/m² by 2100.
We compare projected sea level rise contributions from the Greenland ice sheet under RCP 4.5 and RCP 8.5.
Under RCP 8.5, the CMIP5 multi-model mean projects a warming of 3.7°C with a likely range of 2.6–4.8°C by 2100.
RCP 6.0 is an intermediate forcing scenario rarely considered in regional impact assessments due to limited model availability.
Permafrost thaw rates under RCP 2.6 remain substantially lower than under RCP 8.5 across Siberia and northern Canada.
SSP1-1.9 is the most stringent mitigation scenario, consistent with the 1.5°C Paris Agreement target.
Under SSP1-2.6, global net-zero CO2 emissions are achieved by mid-century in most integrated assessment models.
SSP2-4.5 represents a middle-of-the-road scenario assuming moderate societal and technological change.
We evaluate projected changes in heat wave frequency across Europe under SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5 using the HeatWave Duration Index.
SSP5-8.5 assumes continued heavy reliance on fossil fuels and results in the highest projected warming among the ScenarioMIP scenarios.
The difference in projected warming between SSP1-2.6 and SSP5-8.5 by 2100 exceeds 3°C in the CMIP6 median.
Under SSP3-7.0, the probability of exceeding 2°C of global warming before 2060 is approximately 50% based on CMIP6 models.
The IPCC AR6 synthesis report assigns medium confidence to the attribution of observed precipitation trends to anthropogenic forcing.
IPCC AR5 projected a global mean sea level rise of 0.26–0.82 m by 2100 under a range of RCP scenarios.
The carbon budget consistent with 1.5°C of warming was updated substantially between IPCC AR5 and IPCC AR6.
IPCC AR6 WG1 Chapter 4 synthesises CMIP6 model projections for the 21st century under all five SSP scenarios.
Based on IPCC AR5 findings, tropical cyclone intensification is very likely to increase under continued greenhouse gas forcing.
The GCM ensemble spread in projected Arctic warming is primarily driven by differences in sea ice-albedo feedback strength.
We derive a process-based emergent constraint on ECS using observational data and a 40-member GCM ensemble.
AOGCMs participating in ScenarioMIP provide the primary source of projections for IPCC AR6 Chapter 4.
ESMs differ from earlier GCMs in their explicit representation of biogeochemical cycles and land surface carbon stocks.
The RCM adds value over driving GCM for precipitation extremes but shows limited added value for large-scale temperature trends.
Multi-model GCM ensembles systematically underestimate observed Arctic sea ice loss rates over 1979–2019.
FGOALS-g3 is the Chinese Academy of Sciences contribution to CMIP6, built on the Flexible Global Ocean-Atmosphere-Land System model.
UKESM1-0-LL includes interactive tropospheric and stratospheric chemistry as part of its fully coupled Earth system configuration.
SAM0-UNICON replaces the convective parameterisation with a unified convection scheme that treats shallow and deep convection simultaneously.
AWI-CM-1-1-MR combines the ECHAM6.3 atmosphere with the FESOM2 unstructured-grid ocean model for improved coastal resolution.
KACE-1-0-G is the Korean contribution to CMIP6, sharing the same atmospheric component as HadGEM3-GC31-LL.
TaiESM1 is the Taiwan Earth System Model, developed by the Academia Sinica Earth System Modelling Center.

value.text: Academia Sinica Earth System Modelling Center

data.text: MRI-ESM2-0 is the Meteorological Research Institute Earth System Model that contributed both historical and ScenarioMIP simulations.
E3SM-1-0 is the U.S. Department of Energy's Energy Exascale Earth System Model optimised for high-performance computing architectures.
Historical simulations from 29 CMIP6 models are used to construct the forced component of the observed temperature record.
We apply delta-change bias correction to temperature and precipitation outputs from five CMIP6 models for hydrological impact assessment.
The CMIP5 and CMIP6 ensembles project consistent reductions in the frequency of atmospheric blocking over Europe under RCP 8.5 and SSP5-8.5.
We use the CMIP6 model spread as a measure of uncertainty in regional temperature projections over the Sahel under SSP2-4.5.
Attribution of the 2021 Pacific Northwest heat dome requires comparison of factual and counterfactual CESM2 simulations.
The ice-sheet model PISM is forced with surface mass balance fields from RACMO2.3, which is itself driven by ERA5 lateral boundary conditions.
RACMO2.3 is a regional atmospheric climate model applied over the Greenland ice sheet to compute surface mass balance.
The NEMO ocean model forms the oceanic component in several CMIP6 models including HadGEM3-GC31-LL and IPSL-CM6A-LR.
We use MAR regional climate model forced by ERA5 to simulate Greenland ice sheet surface melt under present-day and future climates.
The ICON model is used in its climate configuration (ICON-ESM) as part of the CMIP6 DECK simulations.
Atmospheric circulation changes in CMIP6 models under SSP3-7.0 show a poleward shift of the subtropical jet stream consistent with theory.
We apply emergent constraints derived from CMIP5 models to constrain the CMIP6 ensemble projection of tropical precipitation change.
SMHI-RCA4 is a regional climate model developed at the Swedish Meteorological and Hydrological Institute and widely used in CORDEX experiments.
We drive SMHI-RCA4 with six CMIP6 models to quantify the uncertainty in projected temperature changes over Scandinavia.
The REMO regional model is used in the CORDEX-Africa domain to downscale outputs from the MPI-ESM1-2-LR global model.
CCLM is a non-hydrostatic regional climate model derived from the COSMO numerical weather prediction model.
High-resolution simulations from HadGEM3-GC31-MM and EC-Earth3P-HR are part of the HighResMIP protocol targeting 25 km atmosphere grids.
We use the Community Land Model CLM5 in offline mode driven by MERRA-2 atmospheric forcing to evaluate soil moisture trends.
The 20CR reanalysis extends the observational record back to 1836 using surface pressure observations and an ensemble Kalman filter.
CMIP6 models show large spread in tropical precipitation sensitivity that is partially attributed to differences in convective parameterisation.
Attribution studies using CMIP6 detection-attribution simulations show that recent global warming is unequivocally due to human influence.
The PRIMAVERA project produced a multi-model ensemble of high-resolution CMIP6-class simulations to study European climate extremes.
PMIP4 coordinates paleo-climate simulations using the same Earth system models participating in CMIP6 under standardised boundary conditions.
We use ScenarioMIP simulations from 18 CMIP6 models to calculate end-of-century heat stress indices under SSP1-2.6 and SSP5-8.5.
CMIP6 models participating in HighResMIP show improved simulation of tropical cyclone tracks compared to standard-resolution CMIP6 models.
The CMIP6 multi-model mean projects a 15–20% reduction in snow water equivalent over the European Alps under SSP5-8.5 by 2080.
